title: Data
text:
As we are working on our project with the population aged 15 and above, we selected to work with the data set Main Labor Force Indicators. This data collection contains monthly data on the labor market in Türkiye.
The data basically illustrates how many people are in the labor force, how many people are employed, the number of people that are unemployed and how many people are not included in the labor force. It also has big percentage numbers like the participation rate of the labor force, the employment rate and the unemployment rate.
Data from January 2005 to February 2026. The data is on a monthly basis and is thus valuable in tracking short-term movements in the employment market.

title: Data
text:
As we are working on our project with the population aged 15 and above, we selected to work with the data set Main Labor Force Indicators. This data collection contains monthly data on the labor market in Türkiye.
The data basically illustrates how many people are in the labor force, how many people are employed, the number of people that are unemployed and how many people are not included in the labor force. It also has big percentage numbers like the participation rate of the labor force, the employment rate and the unemployment rate.
Data from January 2005 to February 2026. The data is on a monthly basis and is thus valuable in tracking short-term movements in the employment market.

title: Data
text:
Source of Data
The data set was obtained from TÜİK Labor Force Statistics. We chose this source since the Turkish statistics Institute (TÜİK) is the official statistics institution of Türkiye. Thus, the data is reliable and appropriate for our purpose.

title: Data
text:
Data Set Variables
The dataset includes a number of key labor market factors.
The first variable is time period. It gives year and month of each observation.
Total population 15 years and over is the total population of persons 15 years and over.
Labor force The number of persons who are either employed or seeking employment.
Employment displays how many people are employed right now.
Unemployment is the amount of persons who are not working yet are actively seeking job.
Not in labor force displays the number of people who are not in the labor market.
Labor force participation rate is the proportion of the population aged 15 years and over that is in the labor force.
Employment rate: percentage of employed people in the population aged 15 and above.
The unemployment rate is the share of jobless people in the labor force.
Some variables are reported in thousands of persons while the rate variables are reported as percentages.

title: Data
section: Why We Chose the Data Set?
text:
Why We Chose the Data Set?
We chose this data set because employment and unemployment are some of the most important indicators of an economy. These statistics provide us an idea of the general state of the labor market.
Another argument for adopting this data is that it is straightforward to understand and may be used for visual analysis. The data is monthly so we can compare months and observe if there are gains or drops in labor market variables.
Our main objective is to present the significant developments in the general labor market situation in Türkiye in a straightforward way.

title: Data
text:
Data Importing
The data was given as an excel file. We loaded the file into R and prepped it for analysis.
The original Excel file has titles, comments, Turkish and English labels and separate parts, therefore it was not instantly ready for analysis. That’s why we first checked the data structure and then cleaned it up.


Code
library(readxl)
raw_data <- read_excel("employement_data.xls", skip = 4, col_names = FALSE)

title: Data
text:
Data Preparation


Code
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(lubridate)

raw_data <- read_excel("employement_data.xls", skip = 4, col_names = FALSE)

base_cols <- c("year", "month_raw", "pop_15_plus", "labor_force", "employment", 
               "unemployment", "not_in_labor_force", "lfp_rate", "emp_rate", "unemp_rate")

df_total <- raw_data[, 1:10]
colnames(df_total) <- base_cols
df_total$gender <- "Total"

df_male <- raw_data[, 12:21] 
colnames(df_male) <- base_cols
df_male$gender <- "Male"

df_female <- raw_data[, 23:32] 
colnames(df_female) <- base_cols
df_female$gender <- "Female"

df_combined <- bind_rows(df_total, df_male, df_female)

df_clean <- df_combined |>
  
  filter(!is.na(month_raw) & !str_detect(month_raw, "Kaynak|Source")) |>
  
  
  fill(year, .direction = "down") |>
  mutate(year = as.numeric(year)) |>
  
  
  separate(month_raw, into = c("month_tr", "month_en"), sep = " - ", extra = "merge") |>
  mutate(
    month_tr = str_trim(month_tr),
    month_en = str_trim(month_en)
  ) |>
  
  
  mutate(
    month_no = case_when(
      month_en == "January" ~ 1, month_en == "February" ~ 2, month_en == "March" ~ 3,
      month_en == "April" ~ 4, month_en == "May" ~ 5, month_en == "June" ~ 6,
      month_en == "July" ~ 7, month_en == "August" ~ 8, month_en == "September" ~ 9,
      month_en == "October" ~ 10, month_en == "November" ~ 11, month_en == "December" ~ 12
    ),
    
    date = make_date(year, month_no, 1)
  ) |>
  
  
  mutate_at(vars(pop_15_plus:unemp_rate), as.numeric) |>
  
  
  select(year, month_no, month_tr, month_en, date, gender, 
         pop_15_plus, labor_force, employment, unemployment, 
         not_in_labor_force, lfp_rate, emp_rate, unemp_rate)


write.csv(df_clean, "labor_final_clean.csv", row.names = FALSE, na = "")


save(df_clean, file = "labor_final_clean.RData")


## AI Usage: Used for creating month variables (lubridate library), seperating the columns of data.
## AI Prompt: "Help me while converting to our xls data to csv, I don't know how to seperate the columns truly."


The first Excel file was not directly good for analysis. Because it have multiple header rows, two language labels, note rows and different parts for Total, Male and Female side by side.
In this step, we import the raw file and deleted unnecessary rows. We separated this three gender blocks and combined them to make one long dataset. The month informations was splitted to Turkish and English names. Also we created a standard date variable. All labor market indicators converted to numeric format.
Finally, we saved the clean dataset as CSV and RData file for use it again in our project.

title: Data
text:
Cleaned Dataset Check


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

labor_data <- read_csv(
  "labor_final_clean.csv",
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")
)

labor_data <- labor_data |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),
    year = as.integer(year),
    month_no = as.integer(month_no),
    gender = factor(gender, levels = c("Total", "Male", "Female")),
    month_en = factor(
      month_en,
      levels = c(
        "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
        "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"
      )
    )
  )


total_data <- labor_data |>
  filter(gender == "Total")


gender_data <- labor_data |>
  filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female"))


yearly_total <- total_data |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarize(
    avg_unemp_rate = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_emp_rate = mean(emp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_lfp_rate = mean(lfp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_employment = mean(employment, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_unemployment = mean(unemployment, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


yearly_gender <- gender_data |>
  group_by(year, gender) |>
  summarize(
    avg_unemp_rate = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_emp_rate = mean(emp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_lfp_rate = mean(lfp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_employment = mean(employment, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_unemployment = mean(unemployment, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )


data_check <- labor_data |>
  summarize(
    number_of_rows = n(),
    number_of_columns = ncol(labor_data),
    first_date = min(date),
    last_date = max(date),
    number_of_genders = n_distinct(gender),
    missing_values = sum(is.na(labor_data))
  )

kable(data_check)

## AI Prompt: I want to check the data's basic structure and also want to show them on our webpage, how can I do this?


After we prepare the dataset, we checked if the clean data have the expected structure. This step summarize number of rows and columns, the date range of data, how many gender categories we have and total missing values.
These checks is helping to confirm that data cleaning process make a consistent and ready to analysis dataset before we move to visualization and interpretation.

title: Data
text:
Variable Dictionary
The variable dictionary defines the columns used in the cleaned data set and clarifies the measurement unit of each labor market indicator.


Code
variable_dictionary <- tibble(
  variable = c(
    "year", "month_no", "month_tr", "month_en", "date", "gender",
    "pop_15_plus", "labor_force", "employment", "unemployment",
    "not_in_labor_force", "lfp_rate", "emp_rate", "unemp_rate"
  ),
  meaning = c(
    "Year of observation",
    "Month number",
    "Month name in Turkish",
    "Month name in English",
    "Monthly date in YYYY-MM-DD format",
    "Gender group: Total, Male, Female",
    "Population aged 15 and over, reported in thousand persons",
    "Number of people in the labor force, reported in thousand persons",
    "Number of employed people, reported in thousand persons",
    "Number of unemployed people, reported in thousand persons",
    "Number of people not included in the labor force, reported in thousand persons",

title: Data
text:
Final Data Set
After cleaning and processing the raw Excel file, the final analysis-ready dataset was saved in .RData format so that it can be reused consistently throughout the project.
Download the final cleaned RData file

text:
Overview
In this page we described what our data set is about, where it comes from, what variables it consists of and how we prepared it for analysis. The following phase of the project we will use this cleaned data set to produce visualizations and discuss the main findings.

title: Team Members
text:
Welcome to our EMU430 Data Analytics final project website.
In this project, we analyze labor-related data to understand key patterns, trends, and insights. Our aim is to present the data clearly, explore important relationships, and summarize meaningful findings through visualizations and data analysis.

Team Members

Erdem Tek
Ömer Caner Parlak
Samet Yüce
Furkan Kaan Dursun
Ezgi Sözdemir
Deniz Asmaz



Project Topic
Project Title: Investigation of Turkish Labor Market (2005-2026)
The subject of our project is about the changes in Turkish job market in the last 20 years. We use the dataset “Main Labour Force Indicators” from TÜİK. The dataset consists of macroeconomic statistics for every month from January 2005 to February 2026.
The main focus of this project is the change of the Turkish economy and the job market over time. Here are the things We want to do: * Check how the major economic shifts (like pre- and post-2020 scenes) impacted employment and unemployment stats. * Compare labor force participation and unemployment rates by gender. * Detect if there are any seasonal patterns in the job market over a year.
We’ll clean the data in R, we’ll create visualizations in ggplot2, and we’ll do some time-series analysis to find trends.


Data Set
Data Source: TÜİK - Main Labor Force Indicators


Key Takeaways
•⁠ ⁠Turkey’s labor market changed significantly between 2005 and 2026, especially during major economic and political periods.
•⁠ ⁠Male employment and labor force participation rates remained consistently higher than female rates.
•⁠ ⁠Gender gaps appear to be structural rather than temporary, especially in labor force participation.
•⁠ ⁠Monthly unemployment averages suggest that there may be seasonal labor market patterns.
•⁠ ⁠The highest unemployment months mostly coincide with periods of economic pressure or transition.


Project Teaser


This video introduces our project topic and summarizes the main purpose of our analysis.



Your browser does not support the video tag. 
:::
:::

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
Key Takeaways
•⁠ ⁠Turkey’s labor market changed significantly between 2005 and 2026, especially during major economic and political periods.
•⁠ ⁠Male employment and labor force participation rates remained consistently higher than female rates.
•⁠ ⁠Gender gaps appear to be structural rather than temporary, especially in labor force participation.
•⁠ ⁠Monthly unemployment averages suggest that there may be seasonal labor market patterns.
•⁠ ⁠The highest unemployment months mostly coincide with periods of economic pressure or transition.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
Setup


Code
library(tidyverse)

labor_data <- read_csv(

labor_data <- labor_data |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date),

      month_en,

        "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
        "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"
      )
    )
  )

total_data <- labor_data |>
  filter(gender == "Total")

gender_data <- labor_data |>
  filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female"))

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
1. General Trend Analysis


Code
yearly_total <- total_data |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarize(
    `Unemployment Rate` = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Employment Rate` = mean(emp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Labor Force Participation Rate` = mean(lfp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

yearly_total_long <- yearly_total |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`Unemployment Rate`, `Employment Rate`, `Labor Force Participation Rate`),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "rate"
  )

ggplot(yearly_total_long, aes(x = year, y = rate)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, color = "#2C5F8A") +
  geom_point(size = 2.4, color = "#2C5F8A") +
  facet_wrap(~ indicator, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Yearly Average Labor Market Rates in Turkey",
    subtitle = "Annual averages calculated from monthly observations, 2005–2026",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Rate (%)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2005, 2026, by = 3)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "none"
  )









We wanted to see how Turkey’s labor market has been in general over the years here. The dataset contains monthly values, which we converted into annual averages. The monthly data has lots of small ups and downs so taking the yearly averages makes the main trend easier to see.
Between 2005 and 2026, the unemployment rate didn’t just go straight up or straight down. In some periods it grew, especially when the economy was going through difficult times. Several of these periods were followed by a falling rate. This can be interpreted as an indication of a partial recovery in the labor market.
We also looked at the unemployment rate and the labor force participation rate. The reason these are helpful is because unemployment alone can give an incomplete picture. For example, unemployment can change when people lose jobs, but it can also change when more people are looking for work or when some stop looking for work.
Thus, this section presents a general overview of the changes over time in the Turkish labor market. That also shows why the unemployment rate alone is not sufficient.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
2. Gender Comparison


Code
yearly_gender <- gender_data |>
  group_by(year, gender) |>
  summarize(
    `Unemployment Rate` = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Employment Rate` = mean(emp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Labor Force Participation Rate` = mean(lfp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

yearly_gender_long <- yearly_gender |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(`Unemployment Rate`, `Employment Rate`, `Labor Force Participation Rate`),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "rate"
  )

ggplot(yearly_gender_long, aes(x = year, y = rate, color = gender)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2) +
  facet_wrap(~ indicator, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Gender-Based Labor Market Comparison in Turkey",
    subtitle = "Yearly averages by gender, 2005–2026",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Rate (%)",
    color = "Gender"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2005, 2026, by = 3)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )









In this section, We compared male and female labor market indicators. The main aim was to see whether labor market conditions differ between men and women.
The graph shows that men generally had higher employment and labor force participation rates than women during the whole period. This suggests that the gender gap in Turkey’s labor market is not temporary. It continued across many years.
The difference is especially clear in labor force participation. In other words, the main issue is not only whether men and women are unemployed, but also whether they are included in the labor force at all.
Because of this, looking only at total labor market indicators can hide some important differences. To understand the labor market better, male and female indicators should be evaluated separately.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
3. Economic and Political Period Analysis


Code
ggplot(total_data, aes(x = date, y = unemp_rate)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2008-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2009-12-31"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, alpha = 0.12, fill = "#D55E00") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2015-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2015-12-31"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, alpha = 0.12, fill = "#0072B2") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2016-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2016-12-31"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, alpha = 0.12, fill = "#CC79A7") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2017-01-01"), xmax = as.Date("2017-12-31"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, alpha = 0.12, fill = "#009E73") +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2020-03-01"), xmax = as.Date("2021-06-30"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, alpha = 0.12, fill = "gray40") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, color = "#1F3B5B") +
  labs(
    title = "Unemployment Rate Across Major Economic and Political Periods",
    subtitle = "Turkey labor market trends between 2005 and 2026",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Unemployment Rate (%)"
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "2 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Code
# AI Usage: Annotating of critic time periods
# AI Prompt: "I want to colorize some of critical time periods for better presentation of graph, how can I do this?"


In this section, we looked at unemployment movements during selected economic and political periods. The shaded areas on the graph represent major periods such as the 2008–2009 global financial crisis, political uncertainty periods, currency pressure, and the COVID-19 period.
The graph shows that unemployment increased clearly around the 2008–2009 global financial crisis. Another noticeable increase can also be seen in the late 2010s and during the COVID-19 period.
However, we should interpret this graph carefully. The shaded areas show a possible relationship, not direct causation. In other words, unemployment movements happened around these major economic and political periods, but the graph alone does not prove that these events were the only reasons for the changes.
Still, this part of the analysis is useful because it connects the labor market indicators with real economic and political events. This makes the unemployment trend easier to understand in a broader context.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
4. Monthly Seasonality Pattern


Code
monthly_pattern <- total_data |>
  group_by(month_no, month_en) |>
  summarize(
    avg_unemp_rate = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(monthly_pattern, aes(x = month_en, y = avg_unemp_rate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2C5F8A", alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_unemp_rate, 1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Unemployment Rate by Month",
    subtitle = "Monthly averages across 2005–2026",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Average Unemployment Rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









In this part, we wanted to see if unemployment changes from month to month. For that reason, we calculated the average unemployment rate for each month by using all years in the dataset.
The graph shows that unemployment is not exactly equal in every month. Some months seem to have a bit higher unemployment, while some others are lower. The difference is not huge, but it is still visible.
One possible reason for this is seasonal work. In Turkey, sectors like tourism, agriculture, and services can create more job opportunities in certain months. Because of this, unemployment may go down in some periods and rise again in others.
So, even though the monthly pattern is not very strong, it still helps us see that unemployment is affected by seasonal factors as well as long-term economic conditions.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
5. Gender Gap Analysis


Code
yearly_gender_gap_data <- labor_data |>
  filter(gender %in% c("Male", "Female")) |>
  group_by(year, gender) |>
  summarize(
    avg_unemp_rate = mean(unemp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_emp_rate = mean(emp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_lfp_rate = mean(lfp_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

gender_gap <- yearly_gender_gap_data |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = gender,
    values_from = c(avg_unemp_rate, avg_emp_rate, avg_lfp_rate)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    unemployment_gap = avg_unemp_rate_Female - avg_unemp_rate_Male,
    employment_gap = avg_emp_rate_Male - avg_emp_rate_Female,
    participation_gap = avg_lfp_rate_Male - avg_lfp_rate_Female
  )

gender_gap_long <- gender_gap |>
  select(year, unemployment_gap, employment_gap, participation_gap) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(unemployment_gap, employment_gap, participation_gap),
    names_to = "gap_type",
    values_to = "gap_value"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    gap_type = recode(
      gap_type,
      unemployment_gap = "Female - Male Unemployment Gap",
      employment_gap = "Male - Female Employment Gap",
      participation_gap = "Male - Female Participation Gap"
    )
  )

ggplot(gender_gap_long, aes(x = year, y = gap_value)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.6, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray45") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, color = "#2C5F8A") +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, color = "#2C5F8A") +
  facet_wrap(~ gap_type, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Gender Gaps in Labor Market Indicators",
    subtitle = "Yearly average differences between male and female labor market rates",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Gap in percentage points"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2005, 2026, by = 3)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









In this part, we focused directly on the difference between men and women in the labor market. Instead of only showing male and female values on the same graph, we also calculated how large the gap is.
The results show that the participation gap stayed high during the whole period. In simple terms, men were much more likely to be in the labor force than women.
A similar pattern can be seen in employment. Male employment rates were higher than female employment rates in almost every year. On the other hand, the unemployment gap changed more from year to year, so it does not show the same stable pattern.
Because of this, the main issue seems to be labor force participation and employment. The gender difference in Turkey’s labor market is not only about unemployment. It is also about how many women are able to enter and stay in the labor force.

title: Analysis and Graphs
text:
6. Highest and Lowest Unemployment Months


Code
highest_unemployment <- total_data |>
  arrange(desc(unemp_rate)) |>
  select(year, month_en, date, unemp_rate, emp_rate, lfp_rate) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

lowest_unemployment <- total_data |>
  arrange(unemp_rate) |>
  select(year, month_en, date, unemp_rate, emp_rate, lfp_rate) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

Code
unemployment_extremes <- bind_rows(
  highest_unemployment |> mutate(group = "Highest Unemployment"),
  lowest_unemployment |> mutate(group = "Lowest Unemployment")
) |>
  mutate(
    period_label = paste(year, month_en),
    period_label = reorder(period_label, unemp_rate)
  )

ggplot(unemployment_extremes, aes(x = period_label, y = unemp_rate, fill = group)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Highest and Lowest Unemployment Months",
    subtitle = "Top 10 highest and lowest monthly unemployment rates, 2005–2026",
    x = "Period",
    y = "Unemployment Rate (%)",
    fill = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 17),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray35"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )









Our next step was to look at the months in the sample that had the highest and lowest unemployment rates. Because of this, we can see the best and worst points more clearly instead of just the averages.
The months with the highest unemployment mostly appeared in periods when the labor market was under pressure. This result is similar to what we saw in the yearly trend and economic period analysis.
The lowest unemployment months show the periods when the labor market was relatively better. When we compare the highest and lowest months, we can see that unemployment in Turkey changed quite a lot between 2005 and 2026.
Overall, this part shows that Turkey’s labor market did not stay the same over time. It was affected by short-term problems, but also by longer-term changes in the labor market.
